content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 2,
1994
May Y.
Doi
Gardena City Clerk
1700 West 162nd Street
Gardena,
CA
90247-3778
RE:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—488
Dear Ms.
Doi:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Because your questions
are general in nature,
we treat your request as one for informal
assistance. 2
QUESTIONS
The City of Gardena has a contribution limitation ordinance.
You have asked the following questions concerning the prohibition
on transfers and its application to candidates for city offices:
1.
Is the transfer ban contained in Government Code Section
85304?
2.
What is the definition of a “transfer”?
3.
Is a candidate or officeholder permitted to use campaign
funds to attend fundraising dinners for other candidates or
officeholders or must they use their personal funds?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The transfer ban is covered under Section 85304.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


May Y.
Doi
1—93—488
Page
2
2.
For purposes of Section 85304,
a “transfer” includes the
giving of campaign funds,
including campaign assets,
and the use
of campaign funds to make non—monetary contributions to any
candidate for elective office who is subject to valid contribution
limitations.
3.
A candidate or officeholder is not permitted to use
campaign funds to attend a fundraiser held by a candidate or
officeholder who is subject to valid contribution limitations.
ANALYSIS
Section 85304 provides:
No candidate for elective office or committee controlled
by that candidate or candidates for elective office shall
transfer any contribution to any other candidate for elective
office.
Transfers of funds between candidates or their
controlled committees are prohibited.
Section 85304 was invalidated by court decision
3 to the
extent the section was interpreted to prohibit circumvention of
fiscal year contribution limits.
Thus,
Section 85304 as applied
to support per election contribution limits remains valid.
Therefore,
Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates and
officeholders from using campaign funds to make contributions or
other payments to other candidates who are subject to valid
contribution limits.
Section 82015 defines “contribution” to include the purchase
of fundraiser tickets.
Therefore,
a candidate or officeholder may
not use campaign funds to purchase tickets to a fundraising event
held by a candidate who is subject to a local contribution limit.
I hope this information has been helpful.
If you need
further assistance, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Linda Moureaux
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
3
Service Employees International Union,
AFL-CIO, et al. v.
Fair Political Practices Commission.
(1992)
955 F.2d 1312.

Doi Gardena City Clerk 1700 West 162nd Street Gardena, CA 90247-3778 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1—93—488 Dear Ms. Doi: You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Because your questions are general in nature, we treat your request as one for informal assistance. 2 QUESTIONS The City of Gardena has a contribution limitation ordinance. You have asked the following questions concerning the prohibition on transfers and its application to candidates for city offices: 1. Is the transfer ban contained in Government Code Section 85304? 2. What is the definition of a “transfer”? 3. Is a candidate or officeholder permitted to use campaign funds to attend fundraising dinners for other candidates or officeholders or must they use their personal funds? CONCLUSIONS 1. The transfer ban is covered under Section 85304. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, g. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 May Y. Doi 1—93—488 Page 2 2. For purposes of Section 85304, a “transfer” includes the giving of campaign funds, including campaign assets, and the use of campaign funds to make non—monetary contributions to any candidate for elective office who is subject to valid contribution limitations. 3. A candidate or officeholder is not permitted to use campaign funds to attend a fundraiser held by a candidate or officeholder who is subject to valid contribution limitations. ANALYSIS Section 85304 provides: No candidate for elective office or committee controlled by that candidate or candidates for elective office shall transfer any contribution to any other candidate for elective office. Transfers of funds between candidates or their controlled committees are prohibited. Section 85304 was invalidated by court decision 3 to the extent the section was interpreted to prohibit circumvention of fiscal year contribution limits. Thus, Section 85304 as applied to support per election contribution limits remains valid. Therefore, Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates and officeholders from using campaign funds to make contributions or other payments to other candidates who are subject to valid contribution limits. Section 82015 defines “contribution” to include the purchase of fundraiser tickets. Therefore, a candidate or officeholder may not use campaign funds to purchase tickets to a fundraising event held by a candidate who is subject to a local contribution limit. I hope this information has been helpful. If you need further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322—5662. Sincerely, Linda Moureaux Political Reform Consultant Technical Assistance Division 3 Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission.
